note: The character of a statutory power a holder may exercise (Annex A6.3). Active from Spiral 2. v0.2 (2026-07-20) added definitions; v0.3 (2026-07-20, sponsor decision) adds `adjudication` and `application_to_court`, closing the 'other' overload that was erasing the judicial layer.
judicial_layer: RESOLVED (sponsor, 2026-07-20). 'other' had been carrying 6 of 20 powers in the Water tranche, including the two most load-bearing records in the special-administration trace - the Secretary of State's petition to the High Court (WIA 1991 s.24(1)(a)) and the Court's own order-making power (s.24(1)). Two families had no term and are now added. They are deliberately kept SEPARATE rather than collapsed into a single 'judicial' term, because they are opposite ends of the same interaction: one actor ASKS a court, another IS the court and decides. Merging them would erase exactly the distinction the delivery chain depends on. The catch-all sense of 'judicial' belongs on the FUNCTIONAL axis instead - a property of the body (see vocab/functions.json `judicial`), which is what a query or a map colour should key on.
typing_rule: Choose the term describing what the holder DOES with the power, not the policy area it operates in. Where two terms fit, prefer the more specific. 'other' requires a `notes` entry saying why no term fits.

terms.definition: Power to require another body or person to act, or not act, in a specified way, binding on the recipient.
Power to confer, vary or withdraw a legal status on a person, body, site or thing.
Power to agree or refuse agreement to another actor's proposed decision. Usually generates a derived Veto record.
Power to approve or withhold approval of a completed decision, scheme, plan or set of rules before it takes effect.
Power to grant, refuse, vary, attach conditions to or revoke an authorisation without which an activity may not lawfully be carried on.
Power to compel compliance with a legal requirement - enforcement notices, orders, undertakings, injunctive steps. Distinguished from `sanction` by aiming at compliance rather than punishment.
Power to enter, examine or survey premises, records or activities.
Power to open and conduct a formal inquiry into a matter, person or body.
Power to make rules of general application - regulations, orders, licence conditions, codes, standards, determinations binding a class.
Power to levy fees, charges or levies to fund functions.
Power to appoint, reappoint or remove a person to or from an office or body.
Power to provide financial support - grants, loans, indemnities, guarantees. Frequently gated by a Treasury consent, which is its own derived Veto.
Power to require the provision of information, documents or explanations.
Power to impose a penalty for non-compliance - financial penalties, censure, licence revocation as punishment. Distinguished from `enforcement` by aiming at punishment rather than compliance.

Power to hear and determine an appeal against another actor's decision. Note this is the power of the appellate BODY; the appellant's right of appeal is what makes the original decision `overridable`. Where the appellate body is a court exercising judicial power, prefer `adjudication`.

Power of a court or tribunal to determine a matter and make a binding order - to grant, refuse, quash or vary. The holder DECIDES; it is not a party. Example: the High Court's power to make a special administration order under WIA 1991 s.24(1).

Power to apply or petition a court for an order. The holder ASKS; the court decides, and may refuse. Distinguished from `adjudication` by which end of the interaction the holder sits at - a distinction the delivery chain depends on. Example: the Secretary of State's power to petition for a special administration order under WIA 1991 s.24(1)(a). A power of this type should normally record the deciding court in `related_body_ids`.
A statutory power whose character matches none of the above. Requires a `notes` entry saying why. See `other_overload` above - a recurring 'other' is a vocabulary gap to be raised, not absorbed.
